text:
GENITOURINARY ONCOLOGY -- ATTENDING PHYSICIAN NOTE
Multidisciplinary Tumor Board Review / Post-Board Oncology Assessment

---

ATTENDING: [GU Oncology Attending]
CC: [Fellow/APP]
REFERRING PROVIDER: [Referring Oncologist]

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Mr. Gerald Hutchinson is a 72-year-old male with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC) who presents for post-tumor board oncology assessment and treatment planning discussion following discontinuation of olaparib due to progressive disease with new visceral metastasis and cumulative treatment toxicity.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Mr. Hutchinson is a 72-year-old retired civil engineer with a significant history of high-risk locally advanced prostate cancer (Gleason 4+5=9, Grade Group 5, cT3bN1M0, AJCC Stage IVA) diagnosed approximately 4-5 years ago with a presenting PSA of 87.4 ng/mL. His disease course has been extensively treated and he now presents at a critical inflection point.

Briefly, following diagnosis, he was initiated on androgen deprivation therapy with leuprolide (Lupron) plus abiraterone (Zytiga) with prednisone, achieving a PSA nadir of 0.4 ng/mL. He received definitive IMRT to the prostate and pelvis (78 Gy / 39 fractions to the primary, 54 Gy to the pelvic nodes), completing approximately 8 weeks of radiation with acceptable acute toxicity (grade 1-2 urinary frequency, mild rectal irritation). He remained on ADT + abiraterone with maintained castrate PSA levels for approximately 24-30 months before developing biochemical progression: serial PSAs of 0.3 → 0.6 → 1.1 → 2.3 ng/mL over 6 months with a PSA doubling time of roughly 3.5 months and confirmed castrate testosterone. At that time, consistent with CRPC criteria, abiraterone was stopped. Germline testing through Myriad myRisk demonstrated a pathogenic BRCA2 frameshift variant (c.5946delT), and somatic NGS via FoundationOne CDx confirmed biallelic BRCA2 loss (LOH), AR amplification (8x), TP53 p.R175H, RB1 deletion, PTEN homozygous deletion, MSS, TMB-low (4 mut/Mb), and elevated genomic LOH at 18%. Enzalutamide (Xtandi) 160 mg daily was initiated as next-line ARSI (second-generation AR signaling inhibitor) given AR amplification without point mutation, with an initial PSA response to 0.7 ng/mL nadir; however, he developed rapid PSA progression approximately 9 months into therapy (PSA doubling time ~6 weeks) with new multifocal bone metastases on restaging bone scan (T10, right posterior 6th rib, left iliac wing, plus previously known right posterior iliac crest lesion). MRI spine confirmed T10 met without cord compromise. He received palliative RT to T10 (30 Gy / 10 fractions) with good pain response. Enzalutamide was discontinued and olaparib (Lynparza) 300 mg BID was initiated given confirmed biallelic BRCA2 loss -- per PROfound trial data -- along with denosumab for bone health.

On olaparib, he demonstrated a meaningful PSA response (nadir 0.6 ng/mL from 2.8 ng/mL at initiation), but was significantly complicated by olaparib-related myelosuppression. He developed grade 3 anemia at month 3 (Hgb nadir 7.1 g/dL), requiring 2 units pRBC and dose reduction to 200 mg BID. Over the subsequent months, he became transfusion-dependent (cumulative 10 units pRBC), with hematology consultation confirming hypocellular marrow with erythroid hypoplasia on bone marrow biopsy -- no marrow infiltration by tumor, consistent with PARP inhibitor toxicity. IV iron (ferric carboxymaltose) was added. He has had persistent grade 2 fatigue, limiting his instrumental ADLs, and his ECOG PS has been 2.

At approximately 6 months of olaparib therapy, restaging CT/bone scan showed stable bone disease but revealed a new 1.4 cm hypoenhancing lesion in segment VII of the liver. CT-guided liver biopsy confirmed metastatic prostatic adenocarcinoma: PSA positive (weak, focal), PSMA strongly positive, NKX3.1 positive, no neuroendocrine features (synaptophysin neg, chromogranin neg, INSM1 neg), Ki-67 35%. PSA began rising at approximately 7-8 months (from nadir 0.6 to 1.8 ng/mL, meeting PSA progression criteria). Olaparib was discontinued.

Most recently, a repeat PSMA PET/CT (68Ga-PSMA-11) was obtained for treatment planning and lutetium-177 PSMA-617 eligibility assessment, demonstrating: 7 PSMA-avid bone metastases (T10, T12, L3, right posterior 6th rib, left iliac wing, right posterior iliac crest, right femoral head), PSMA-avid right iliac chain lymphadenopathy (1.3 cm, SUVmax 6.1), and the hepatic segment VII lesion (now 1.8 cm, SUVmax 8.4). No CNS disease on skull survey. Overall PSMA expression described as high across all lesions (SUVmax range 3.8-8.4).

Case was presented at GU Oncology Tumor Board today with the following discussion:
- Patient has progressed through ADT/abiraterone, enzalutamide, and olaparib -- three prior lines in the CRPC setting
- Has not received taxane-based chemotherapy (cabazitaxel-naive, docetaxel-naive)
- PSMA PET demonstrates robust expression across all disease sites, meeting eligibility criteria for Pluvicto (lutetium-177 PSMA-617)
- VISION trial required prior ARSI and prior taxane; patient has not received taxane, but given his ECOG 2, symptomatic anemia, significant cumulative toxicity burden, and patient's strong preference to avoid cytotoxic chemotherapy, the multidisciplinary team (GU oncology, radiation oncology, palliative care) agreed that proceeding to lutetium-177 PSMA-617 is appropriate in the context of a risk-benefit discussion
- Cabazitaxel remains an option but was discussed with patient who declined cytotoxic chemotherapy at this time
- Clinical trial enrollment was reviewed -- patient does not meet criteria for currently enrolling studies at this time
- Palliative care is actively co-managing

Today Mr. Hutchinson presents accompanied by his wife. He endorses ongoing fatigue (grade 2, worse with exertion), decreased appetite with an estimated 8-10 lb unintentional weight loss over the past 2 months, and intermittent right upper quadrant discomfort. The back pain from his T10 met has notably improved following radiation. He denies new neurological symptoms, lower extremity weakness or numbness, bowel or bladder dysfunction, dyspnea at rest, or hemoptysis.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
Positive: fatigue (grade 2), decreased appetite, mild RUQ discomfort, prior mid-back pain (improved), occasional nocturia (residual post-radiation), intermittent mild nausea.
Negative: no fever, no chills, no chest pain, no dyspnea at rest, no cough, no hemoptysis, no headache, no vision changes, no extremity weakness or sensory deficits, no lower extremity edema, no gross hematuria, no melena, no hematemesis.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY
1. Metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (as above)
2. Hypertension -- on medications, controlled
3. Type 2 diabetes mellitus -- managed with oral agents (metformin; no insulin)
4. Former smoker (20 pack-year history, quit age 55)
5. Olaparib-induced anemia, transfusion-dependent -- ongoing

PAST SURGICAL / PROCEDURAL HISTORY
- Transrectal ultrasound-guided prostate needle biopsy (at diagnosis)
- CT-guided liver biopsy, segment VII (approximately 1 month ago)
- Bone marrow biopsy (hematology, approximately 1-2 months ago)
- Multiple red blood cell transfusions (cumulative ~10 units pRBC)
- No prior radical prostatectomy

SOCIAL HISTORY
Retired civil engineer. Lives with his wife who is a primary caregiver. Non-smoker (former, quit age 55). Rare alcohol use. No illicit drug use. Functionally, currently ECOG PS 2 (symptomatic, in bed <50% of day).

FAMILY HISTORY
Father: prostate cancer diagnosed at age 65
Paternal uncle: deceased from prostate cancer
No known history of breast, ovarian, or pancreatic cancer in first-degree relatives
Germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant confirmed in patient; genetic counseling referral placed, cascade testing recommended for family members

ALLERGIES
NKDA per chart (no known drug allergies)

MEDICATIONS (current)
1. Leuprolide acetate (Lupron Depot) 22.5 mg IM q3 months -- ongoing ADT
2. Denosumab (Xgeva) 120 mg SQ q4 weeks
3. Calcium carbonate 500 mg PO BID (with denosumab)
4. Vitamin D3 2000 IU PO daily
5. Metformin 1000 mg PO BID (T2DM)
6. Lisinopril 10 mg PO daily (hypertension)
7. Ondansetron 4 mg PO q8h PRN nausea
8. Prednisone 5 mg PO BID -- being tapered off (was on with abiraterone, tapering per plan)
9. Olaparib -- DISCONTINUED at prior visit

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
Vitals: BP 128/74 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, RR 16, Temp 98.4°F, SpO2 97% on room air, Weight 164 lbs (down from 172 lbs approximately 2 months ago)
General: Pleasant male, appears mildly fatigued but in no acute distress. Alert and oriented x3.
HEENT: Anicteric sclerae, mucous membranes moist, no oral lesions.
Neck: No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs. No JVD.
Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes or crackles.
Abdomen: Soft, mildly tender to deep palpation in the RUQ without guarding or rebound. Liver edge palpable approximately 2 cm below the right costal margin. Spleen not palpable. No ascites.
Extremities: No lower extremity edema. No calf tenderness bilaterally. Skin with no rash or ecchymosis.
Muscuoloskeletal: Midline spinal tenderness at approximately T10 (site of known met, post-RT), otherwise no focal bony tenderness. No paraspinal muscle spasm at this time.
Neurological: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Motor strength 5/5 upper and lower extremities bilaterally. Sensation intact to light touch. Gait steady, mildly slow. No focal neurological deficits.
Genitourinary: No scrotal/testicular masses. Digital rectal exam deferred (previously without palpable nodularity; patient recently biopsied).

---

LABORATORY RESULTS
PSA: 1.8 ng/mL (rising from nadir 0.6 ng/mL; PSA progression confirmed)
Testosterone: <10 ng/dL (castrate confirmed)
CBC: WBC 4.1 K/mcL, Hemoglobin 8.3 g/dL (grade 2 anemia, post-transfusion day ~14), Hematocrit 25.1%, MCV 88 fL, Platelets 211 K/mcL
Reticulocyte %: 0.9% (reticulocyte index <1, hypoproliferative)
CMP: Sodium 138, Potassium 4.1, Creatinine 1.1 mg/dL (eGFR ~66 mL/min/1.73m2), BUN 22, ALT 38 U/L, AST 44 U/L (mildly elevated, likely related to hepatic met), ALP 112 U/L, Total bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL, Albumin 3.4 g/dL (mild hypoalbuminemia, nutritional)
LDH: 248 U/L (mildly elevated)
PT/INR: 1.1 (normal)
Calcium: 9.1 mg/dL (normal, on supplementation with denosumab)

---

IMAGING RESULTS
Most recent PSMA PET/CT (68Ga-PSMA-11): as detailed in HPI above -- 7 PSMA-avid bone lesions, right iliac chain lymph node (SUVmax 6.1), hepatic segment VII lesion (SUVmax 8.4, 1.8 cm). High PSMA expression across all sites. No CNS involvement.
Most recent CT chest/abdomen/pelvis: stable bone disease, new hepatic lesion (now confirmed met by biopsy), no pulmonary metastases, no spinal cord compromise.
MRI thoracic/lumbar spine (prior): T10 and L3 osseous involvement, no epidural extension, no cord compression confirmed.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Mr. Hutchinson is a 72-year-old male with mCRPC, germline and somatic BRCA2 loss, who has progressed through multiple lines of therapy including ADT + abiraterone (with definitive RT), enzalutamide, and olaparib, now with confirmed visceral (hepatic) metastases, widespread PSMA-avid osseous disease, and significant treatment-related morbidity (transfusion-dependent anemia, ECOG PS 2). Case reviewed and discussed at GU Oncology Multidisciplinary Tumor Board today.

1. mCRPC -- Progression on olaparib with visceral metastasis / Next-line therapy
Following multidisciplinary tumor board discussion, the recommended next-line therapy is lutetium-177 PSMA-617 (Pluvicto, 177Lu-PSMA-617). The basis for this recommendation:
- Confirmed high PSMA expression on 68Ga-PSMA-11 PET/CT across all disease sites (SUVmax 3.8-8.4), satisfying expression threshold for radioligand therapy
- Three prior lines of systemic therapy in CRPC setting (abiraterone, enzalutamide, olaparib)
- Patient has not received taxane-based chemotherapy; cabazitaxel was discussed at length but patient declines cytotoxic chemotherapy at this time, which is his right given the palliative intent of all therapy at this stage
- Significant comorbidities limiting chemotherapy tolerance: ECOG PS 2, transfusion-dependent anemia, hepatic metastases
- PSMA-based radioligand therapy (VISION trial regimen): 7.4 GBq (200 mCi) IV q6 weeks x 6 cycles. Patient was counseled on mechanism of action, expected response rates (PSA response in ~46% per VISION, OS benefit), potential side effects (fatigue, dry mouth/xerostomia, nausea, hematologic toxicity -- particularly relevant given his pre-existing anemia), and radiation safety precautions
- Pre-treatment requirements: creatinine/renal function adequate (eGFR ~66), no salivary gland exclusion criteria, no bowel obstruction
- Will coordinate with nuclear medicine / theranostics program for treatment scheduling
- Referral to nuclear medicine / Pluvicto treatment program placed today
- ADT (leuprolide) to continue throughout -- testosterone must remain castrate

2. Anemia -- transfusion-dependent, olaparib-related
Hemoglobin 8.3 g/dL post-transfusion approximately 2 weeks ago. Olaparib now discontinued, which should allow marrow recovery over the coming weeks. Will monitor CBC weekly x 4 weeks then biweekly. Anticipate slow hematopoietic recovery given marrow biopsy-confirmed erythroid hypoplasia. Transfusion threshold: maintain Hgb >8.0 g/dL; if symptomatic or Hgb <7.5, transfuse 1-2 units pRBC. Continuing IV iron supplementation (ferric carboxymaltose). EPO-stimulating agents remain deferred; with PSMA-targeted therapy imminent, will reassess in collaboration with hematology. Continue denosumab with calcium/vitamin D.

3. Bone metastases / skeletal health
Patient has 7 known osseous metastases. Post-T10 RT with pain response, no new focal bony pain today. L3 involvement -- no neurological compromise at this time. Continue denosumab 120 mg SQ q4 weeks. Counsel patient on signs/symptoms of spinal cord compression and instructed to go to ER immediately with any new leg weakness, numbness, bowel/bladder incontinence. No new RT indicated at this time.

4. Hepatic metastasis
Biopsy-confirmed hepatic met, segment VII (1.8 cm on most recent PSMA PET/CT). Mildly elevated LFTs (AST 44, ALT 38, ALP 112) likely attributable. No current signs of hepatic decompensation (bilirubin normal, INR normal, albumin only mildly low). Will monitor LFTs at each visit. If hepatic disease progresses on lutetium-177, will revisit options including consideration of liver-directed approaches in consultation with IR/surgical oncology, though this remains investigational in the prostate context.

5. Nutrition / Performance status / Symptom management
8-10 lb weight loss over 2 months, albumin 3.4. Referral to oncology dietitian placed. Patient offered nutritional supplementation (Ensure, Boost). Ondansetron continued PRN nausea. Palliative care team actively co-managing -- ongoing goals of care and symptom management. Discussed prognosis honestly with patient and wife in the context of mCRPC with visceral metastases and prior multi-line therapy. Patient understands the disease is not curable but expresses strong wish to pursue further treatment for disease control and quality of life maintenance. Goals are aligned with disease-directed palliative therapy.

6. Diabetes / Hypertension
Continue metformin and lisinopril. Blood pressure well controlled today. Glucose management: will continue to monitor; note that prednisone being weaned (was co-administered with abiraterone, should no longer be required), which may help with glucose control.

7. Prednisone taper
Prednisone 5 mg BID was co-prescribed with abiraterone. Abiraterone was stopped at CRPC progression months ago. Taper prednisone: 5 mg daily x 2 weeks, then discontinue. Patient counseled on signs of adrenal insufficiency (unlikely given short course, but to be aware).

8. Follow-up
Return to clinic in 3-4 weeks, ideally following scheduling of Pluvicto initiation with nuclear medicine. CBC to be checked at 2-week interval. PSA, CMP, LFTs at next visit. Patient instructed to call with worsening pain, new neurologic symptoms, shortness of breath, or if requiring transfusion before scheduled visit.

Total face-to-face time with patient and family: approximately 55 minutes (>50% time in counseling and coordination of care).

[Electronically signed by Attending, GU Oncology]
